linear algebra conductance matrix.summary: The conductance matrix is a diagonal positive definite matrix whose entries are the reciprocals of the wire resistances in a network.

linear algebra discrete gradient diverge.path: linear algebra/linear algebra discrete gradient divergence duality.md
linear algebra discrete gradient diverge.summary: In a spring-mass chain, the incidence matrix $A$ computes discrete differences (a gradient), while its transpose $A^T$ computes discrete sums (a divergence), and together they produce the tridiagonal stiffness matrix $K = A^T C A$.

linear algebra electrical mechanical cor.path: linear algebra/linear algebra electrical mechanical correspondence.md
linear algebra electrical mechanical cor.summary: The equilibrium equations for an electrical resistive network and a mechanical mass–spring structure are mathematically identical, differing only in the physical meaning of each variable and matrix.

linear algebra electrical network graph.path: linear algebra/linear algebra electrical network graph model.md
linear algebra electrical network graph.summary: An electrical network of wires and junction points is modeled as a weighted directed graph where nodes are junctions and edges are wires carrying resistance weights.

linear algebra truss stiffness matrix.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss stiffness matrix.md
linear algebra truss stiffness matrix.summary: The stiffness matrix $K$ of a truss is the symmetric positive semidefinite matrix that directly relates nodal displacements to the external forces needed to maintain those displacements at equilibrium.

linear algebra truss stability theorem.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss stability theorem.md
linear algebra truss stability theorem.summary: A truss can resist any external loading without collapsing if and only if the only displacement that stretches no bar is the zero displacement — in other words, the reduced incidence matrix has no nontrivial kernel.

linear algebra equilibrium potential ene.path: linear algebra/linear algebra equilibrium potential energy.md
linear algebra equilibrium potential ene.summary: The total energy stored in a spring-mass system as a quadratic function of displacements, whose unique minimum point is exactly the equilibrium configuration.

linear algebra gram matrix positive defi.path: linear algebra/linear algebra gram matrix positive definiteness.md
linear algebra gram matrix positive defi.summary: Any matrix of the form $A^T C A$ with $C$ positive definite and $A$ full column rank is automatically positive definite — this is the algebraic engine behind why equilibrium systems have unique solutions.

linear algebra hooke law.summary: A spring's internal force is proportional to how much it has been stretched or compressed, with a positive stiffness constant that measures how hard the spring resists deformation.

linear algebra graph laplacian.summary: The graph Laplacian is a matrix built from a graph's structure that generalizes the second-derivative operator to networks, with diagonal entries equal to vertex degrees and off-diagonal entries equal to $-1$ for adjacent vertices.

linear algebra kirchhoff current law.path: linear algebra/linear algebra kirchhoff current law.md
linear algebra kirchhoff current law.summary: At every node in a network, the total current flowing out along wires equals the external current being injected at that node — no charge accumulates.

linear algebra kirchhoff voltage law.path: linear algebra/linear algebra kirchhoff voltage law.md
linear algebra kirchhoff voltage law.summary: The sum of voltages around any closed loop in a network is zero, which is the statement that voltage is a potential difference and not path-dependent.

linear algebra mass-spring chain.summary: A chain of masses connected by springs, analyzed as a linear system to find how far each mass moves when external forces are applied.

linear algebra network battery source.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network battery source.md
linear algebra network battery source.summary: A battery in a network is a voltage source along a wire that shifts the voltage equation by a fixed amount, and its effect is equivalent to injecting specific current sources at the wire's two endpoints.

linear algebra network equilibrium solva.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network equilibrium solvability condition.md
linear algebra network equilibrium solva.summary: A connected electrical network can reach a steady state only if the total current being injected equals the total current being extracted — the net current into the network must be zero.

linear algebra network equilibrium syste.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network equilibrium system.md
linear algebra network equilibrium syste.summary: The three laws governing an electrical network (voltage law, Ohm's law, current law) combine into a single linear system relating node voltages to external current sources.

linear algebra network grounding.summary: Grounding a node means fixing its voltage potential to zero, which eliminates the free parameter in the voltage solution and makes the reduced system uniquely solvable.

linear algebra network power minimizatio.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network power minimization.md
linear algebra network power minimizatio.summary: An electrical network at equilibrium distributes voltage potentials so as to minimize the total power dissipated as heat across all wires.

linear algebra network resistivity matri.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network resistivity matrix.md
linear algebra network resistivity matri.summary: The resistivity matrix combines the network's topology and wire conductances into a single matrix whose entries encode how strongly each pair of nodes is electrically coupled.

linear algebra network voltage potential.path: linear algebra/linear algebra network voltage potential ambiguity.md
linear algebra network voltage potential.summary: Absolute voltage potentials at network nodes are not uniquely determined — only their differences are physical — so the equilibrium system has infinitely many solutions that all produce identical, unique currents and wire voltages.

linear algebra ohm law matrix form.path: linear algebra/linear algebra ohm law matrix form.md
linear algebra ohm law matrix form.summary: The current through each wire is proportional to the voltage across it, with proportionality constant equal to the wire's conductance (reciprocal of resistance).

linear algebra reduced incidence matrix.path: linear algebra/linear algebra reduced incidence matrix.md
linear algebra reduced incidence matrix.summary: A matrix that encodes which masses each spring connects, including the fixed boundary supports, so that spring elongations can be computed from mass displacements by a single matrix multiply.

linear algebra statically determinate sy.path: linear algebra/linear algebra statically determinate system.md
linear algebra statically determinate sy.summary: A mechanical system where the internal forces can be found directly from the force-balance equations, without needing to know material stiffnesses or solve for displacements first.

linear algebra statically indeterminate.path: linear algebra/linear algebra statically indeterminate system.md
linear algebra statically indeterminate.summary: A mechanical system in which the internal forces cannot be determined from the force-balance equations alone — you must first solve for the displacements using the full stiffness system.

linear algebra stiffness matrix.summary: The matrix $K = A^T C A$ that converts mass displacements directly into the forces needed to hold the system in equilibrium.

linear algebra structural mechanics elec.path: linear algebra/linear algebra structural mechanics electrical network analogy.md
linear algebra structural mechanics elec.summary: Trusses and resistor networks are governed by the same three-equation mathematical skeleton: a gradient law, a constitutive law, and a divergence (balance) law — with every quantity having a direct counterpart in both worlds.

linear algebra truss bar elongation line.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss bar elongation linear approximation.md
linear algebra truss bar elongation line.summary: When a bar's endpoints move by small amounts, the change in the bar's length can be well approximated by a simple dot product formula instead of the exact nonlinear square-root expression.

linear algebra truss hooke law constitut.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss hooke law constitutive relation.md
linear algebra truss hooke law constitut.summary: Each bar in a truss acts like a spring: its internal force is proportional to how much it has been stretched or compressed.

linear algebra truss incidence matrix.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss incidence matrix.md
linear algebra truss incidence matrix.summary: The incidence matrix of a truss is a rectangular array that encodes, for each bar, how much each node's displacement contributes to that bar's elongation.

linear algebra truss mechanism.summary: A mechanism is a non-rigid deformation of a structure — a way the nodes can move and change shape without stretching or compressing any bar, even after the structure has been grounded to remove rigid motions.

linear algebra truss rigid motion kernel.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss rigid motion kernel.md
linear algebra truss rigid motion kernel.summary: The kernel of a truss's incidence matrix consists of all displacement patterns that move the structure as a rigid body — translations and (linearized) rotations — without stretching any bar.

linear algebra truss statically determin.path: linear algebra/linear algebra truss statically determinate vs indeterminate.md
linear algebra truss statically determin.summary: A truss is statically determinate if the internal bar forces can be computed directly from force balance alone (without knowing displacements), and statically indeterminate if one must first solve for displacements via the full stiffness system.

linear algebra 2x2 determinant.summary: The determinant of a $2 \times 2$ matrix is a single number that tells you whether the matrix is invertible and, if so, by how much it scales areas.

numerical methods thomas algorithm.summary: The Thomas algorithm is a specialized form of Gaussian Elimination that solves a tridiagonal linear system in $O(n)$ operations by exploiting the fact that only the three central diagonals are nonzero.

numerical methods strassen matrix multip.path: numerical methods/numerical methods strassen matrix multiplication.md
numerical methods strassen matrix multip.summary: An algorithm that multiplies two $n \times n$ matrices using $n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$ multiplications instead of the naive $n^3$, by recursively splitting matrices into four blocks and replacing 8 block-multiplications with 7 cleverly chosen ones.

linear algebra tridiagonal matrix.summary: A square matrix where every entry is zero except possibly those on the main diagonal and the two adjacent diagonals directly above and below it.

linear algebra vector.summary: A vector is a single column (or row) of numbers that represents a point, a direction, or a list of unknowns in one package.

linear algebra tridiagonal matrix lu fac.path: linear algebra/linear algebra tridiagonal matrix lu factorization.md
linear algebra tridiagonal matrix lu fac.summary: The $LU$ factorization of a tridiagonal matrix produces a lower bidiagonal $L$ and an upper bidiagonal $U$, and the entire factorization requires only $O(n)$ operations instead of $O(n^3)$.

linear algebra triangular matrix inverse.path: linear algebra/linear algebra triangular matrix inverse.md
linear algebra triangular matrix inverse.summary: The inverse of a triangular matrix with nonzero diagonal entries is itself triangular of the same type, and the inverse of a unitriangular matrix is again unitriangular.

linear algebra triangular form.summary: A linear system is in triangular form when each successive equation involves one fewer unknown than the one above it, so the last equation has only one unknown and the system can be solved from the bottom up.

linear algebra transpose inverse commuta.path: linear algebra/linear algebra transpose inverse commutativity.md
linear algebra transpose inverse commuta.summary: For an invertible matrix, the operations of transposing and inverting can be applied in either order and give the same result.

linear algebra symmetric skew-symmetric.path: linear algebra/linear algebra symmetric skew-symmetric decomposition.md
linear algebra symmetric skew-symmetric.summary: Every square matrix can be split into exactly one symmetric part and one skew-symmetric part that sum back to the original.

linear algebra symmetric matrix.summary: A square matrix is symmetric if it looks the same after you swap its rows and columns.

linear algebra augmented matrix.summary: An augmented matrix packs the coefficient matrix and the right-hand side vector of a linear system side by side into one array, so that row operations affect both simultaneously.

linear algebra back substitution.summary: The process of solving a triangular system by starting from the last equation (which has one unknown), finding its value, then working upward and substituting known values into each equation above.

linear algebra banded matrix.summary: A square matrix where all nonzero entries are confined to a diagonal strip of fixed width $k$ around the main diagonal, with zeros everywhere outside that band.

linear algebra basic and free variables.path: linear algebra/linear algebra basic and free variables.md
linear algebra basic and free variables.summary: After row-reducing a linear system, variables attached to pivot columns are called basic (their values are forced once you pick everything else), and variables in non-pivot columns are called free (you can set them to anything you like).

linear algebra compatibility condition.summary: A linear system has at least one solution if and only if no row-reduction step ever produces a zero row on the left side paired with a nonzero entry on the right side — that pairing would demand $0 = c$ for some $c \neq 0$.

linear algebra cramer rule.summary: Cramer's rule gives a formula for each variable of a nonsingular linear system as a ratio of two determinants.

linear algebra determinant inverse formu.path: linear algebra/linear algebra determinant inverse formula.md
linear algebra determinant inverse formu.summary: The determinant of an inverse matrix is the reciprocal of the original matrix's determinant.

linear algebra determinant product formu.path: linear algebra/linear algebra determinant product formula.md
linear algebra determinant product formu.summary: The determinant of a product of two square matrices equals the product of their individual determinants.

linear algebra determinant row operation.path: linear algebra/linear algebra determinant row operation axioms.md
linear algebra determinant row operation.summary: Three rules describe exactly how each type of elementary row operation changes the determinant of a matrix.

linear algebra determinant transpose inv.path: linear algebra/linear algebra determinant transpose invariance.md
linear algebra determinant transpose inv.summary: Transposing a matrix — flipping it across its diagonal — does not change its determinant.

linear algebra determinant via gaussian.path: linear algebra/linear algebra determinant via gaussian elimination.md
linear algebra determinant via gaussian.summary: You compute a matrix's determinant by running Gaussian Elimination and multiplying the pivots together, adjusting the sign for any row swaps made along the way.

linear algebra determinant.summary: The determinant is a single number attached to any square matrix that tells you whether the matrix is invertible.

linear algebra diagonal matrix.summary: A diagonal matrix is a square matrix where every entry off the main diagonal is zero, so only the diagonal entries can be nonzero.

linear algebra elementary matrix type 3.path: linear algebra/linear algebra elementary matrix type 3.md
linear algebra elementary matrix type 3.summary: An elementary matrix of type 3 is the identity matrix with one diagonal entry replaced by a nonzero scalar, and it scales a single row of any matrix it left-multiplies.

linear algebra elementary matrix.summary: An elementary matrix is the identity matrix with one off-diagonal entry changed; multiplying by it on the left performs the corresponding row operation on any matrix.

linear algebra equivalent system.summary: Two linear systems are equivalent when every solution of one is also a solution of the other — they look different on paper but describe exactly the same set of answers.

linear algebra forward substitution.summary: Forward substitution solves a lower triangular system by computing unknowns in order from the first to the last, substituting each found value into subsequent equations.

linear algebra full pivoting.summary: At each step of Gaussian Elimination, swap both rows and columns to move the absolutely largest entry in the remaining submatrix into the pivot position.

linear algebra gauss-jordan elimination.summary: Gauss-Jordan elimination is an algorithm that computes the inverse of a matrix by augmenting it with an identity matrix and row-reducing until the left side becomes the identity, at which point the right side is the inverse.

linear algebra gaussian elimination oper.path: linear algebra/linear algebra gaussian elimination operation count.md
linear algebra gaussian elimination oper.summary: Counting the exact number of multiplications and additions that Gaussian Elimination needs to reduce an $n \times n$ matrix to upper triangular form.

linear algebra gaussian elimination.summary: A step-by-step procedure that simplifies any system of linear equations into triangular form by repeatedly adding multiples of one equation to another, then reads off the solution by back-substitution.

linear algebra homogeneous system.summary: A homogeneous linear system is one where every right-hand side equals zero, so the question is not whether a solution exists but how many solutions there are beyond the obvious all-zeros answer.

linear algebra identity matrix.summary: The identity matrix is the square matrix that leaves every other matrix unchanged when multiplied by it, playing the role of the number $1$ in matrix arithmetic.

linear algebra ill-conditioned matrix.summary: An ill-conditioned matrix is one that is "almost singular" in the sense that tiny changes to the right-hand side or to matrix entries produce enormous changes in the solution.

linear algebra inverse solution formula.path: linear algebra/linear algebra inverse solution formula.md
linear algebra inverse solution formula.summary: When the coefficient matrix of a linear system is invertible, the unique solution can be written as the inverse matrix times the right-hand side vector.

linear algebra solution existence and un.path: linear algebra/linear algebra solution existence and uniqueness theorem.md
linear algebra solution existence and un.summary: A system of linear equations has either exactly one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solution at all — no other count is possible.

linear algebra singular matrix.summary: A square matrix is singular if Gaussian Elimination with row swaps gets permanently stuck — at some column, both the diagonal entry and every entry below it are zero.

linear algebra skew-symmetric matrix.summary: A skew-symmetric matrix is a square matrix that equals the negative of its own transpose, forcing all diagonal entries to be zero.

linear algebra similar matrices determin.path: linear algebra/linear algebra similar matrices determinant.md
linear algebra similar matrices determin.summary: Two matrices that represent the same linear transformation in different bases always have the same determinant.

linear algebra scalar multiplication.summary: Scalar multiplication stretches or shrinks every entry of a matrix by the same real number.

linear algebra sherman morrison woodbury.path: linear algebra/linear algebra sherman morrison woodbury formula.md
linear algebra sherman morrison woodbury.summary: The Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury formula gives an exact closed-form expression for the inverse of a matrix that has been modified by a low-rank update, without recomputing the full inverse from scratch.

linear algebra row operation.summary: A row operation is an allowed manipulation of a linear system's equations — adding a multiple of one equation to another, swapping two equations, or scaling an equation — that never changes which solutions the system has.

linear algebra row echelon form.path: linear algebra/linear algebra row echelon form.md
linear algebra row echelon form.summary: A matrix is in row echelon form when it has a staircase structure: each row's first nonzero entry (the pivot) sits strictly to the right of the pivot in the row above, and all rows of zeros sink to the bottom.

linear algebra regular matrix.summary: A square matrix is regular if Gaussian elimination can reduce it to upper triangular form without ever encountering a zero on the diagonal.

linear algebra rectangular lu factorizat.path: linear algebra/linear algebra rectangular lu factorization.md
linear algebra rectangular lu factorizat.summary: The $PA = LU$ factorization generalizes from square nonsingular matrices to any rectangular matrix, where $U$ is now a row echelon matrix rather than a square upper triangular one.

linear algebra pivoting.summary: Pivoting is the act of swapping two rows of a matrix during Gaussian Elimination so that a nonzero entry moves into the current diagonal position, allowing elimination to continue.

linear algebra pivot.summary: A pivot is the leading nonzero entry in the current working row during Gaussian elimination — it is the number you divide by to zero out all entries below it in the same column.

linear algebra permuted lu decomposition.path: linear algebra/linear algebra permuted lu decomposition.md
linear algebra permuted lu decomposition.summary: The permuted LU decomposition rewrites any nonsingular matrix as a permutation of its rows times a lower triangular matrix times an upper triangular matrix, extending LU factorization to matrices that require row swaps during elimination.

linear algebra permutation matrix.summary: A permutation matrix is a rearrangement of the identity matrix's rows that, when multiplied on the left of any matrix, reorders that matrix's rows in the same way.

linear algebra permutation group matrix.path: linear algebra/linear algebra permutation group matrix correspondence.md
linear algebra permutation group matrix.summary: Every rearrangement of a finite ordered list corresponds to exactly one permutation matrix, and composing two rearrangements corresponds to multiplying their matrices — the two structures are the same object in different clothes.

linear algebra permutation expansion det.path: linear algebra/linear algebra permutation expansion determinant.md
linear algebra permutation expansion det.summary: The determinant can be written as a sum over all ways to choose one entry from each row and each column, each term signed according to whether that selection corresponds to an even or odd rearrangement of the rows.

linear algebra partial pivoting.summary: At each step of Gaussian Elimination, swap the current row with whichever row below it has the largest absolute value in the pivot column, before performing the elimination.

linear algebra nonsingular matrix.summary: A square matrix is nonsingular if Gaussian Elimination with row swaps can reduce it to upper triangular form with every diagonal entry nonzero.

linear algebra nonsingular matrix elemen.path: linear algebra/linear algebra nonsingular matrix elementary matrix factorization.md
linear algebra nonsingular matrix elemen.summary: Every invertible matrix can be written as a product of elementary matrices — the ones that perform the basic row operations used in Gaussian elimination.

linear algebra matrix.summary: A matrix is a rectangular grid of numbers arranged in rows and columns, used to organize and compute with structured data.

linear algebra matrix transpose.summary: The transpose of a matrix is a new matrix formed by swapping every row with its corresponding column.

linear algebra matrix trace.summary: The trace of a square matrix is the sum of its diagonal entries, a single number that captures a fundamental invariant of the matrix.

linear algebra matrix similarity.summary: Two matrices are similar if one can be obtained from the other by changing the basis — multiplying by an invertible matrix on one side and its inverse on the other.

linear algebra matrix multiplication.summary: Matrix multiplication combines two matrices by taking dot products of every row of the first with every column of the second to produce a new matrix.

linear algebra matrix rank.summary: The rank of a matrix is the number of pivots that appear when you row-reduce it to echelon form — it counts how many independent constraints the matrix actually enforces.

linear algebra matrix inverse.summary: The inverse of a square matrix is another matrix that, when multiplied with the original on either side, produces the identity matrix.

linear algebra matrix inverse product ru.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix inverse product rule.md
linear algebra matrix inverse product ru.summary: The inverse of a product of matrices is the product of their individual inverses, but taken in the reverse order.

linear algebra matrix inverse uniqueness.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix inverse uniqueness.md
linear algebra matrix inverse uniqueness.summary: A square matrix can have at most one inverse — there is never a choice between two different inverses.

linear algebra matrix inverse inefficien.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix inverse inefficiency.md
linear algebra matrix inverse inefficien.summary: Computing the matrix inverse $A^{-1}$ to solve $Ax = b$ requires roughly three times as many arithmetic operations as simply using Gaussian Elimination with Back Substitution.

linear algebra matrix form of linear sys.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix form of linear system.md
linear algebra matrix form of linear sys.summary: The matrix form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ rewrites an entire system of linear equations as a single equation between two vectors, compressing all the equations and all the coefficients into three symbols.

linear algebra matrix factorization.summary: Writing a matrix as a product of simpler, more structured matrices so that computations with it become easier or more revealing.

linear algebra matrix commutator.summary: The commutator of two matrices is the difference between their products in the two possible orders, measuring exactly how much and in what direction the two matrices fail to commute.

linear algebra matrix commutativity fail.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix commutativity failure.md
linear algebra matrix commutativity fail.summary: Unlike ordinary numbers, matrix multiplication is not commutative: switching the order of two matrices generally produces a different result, or may not even be defined.

linear algebra matrix column combination.path: linear algebra/linear algebra matrix column combination.md
linear algebra matrix column combination.summary: Multiplying a matrix $A$ by a vector $\mathbf{x}$ produces a linear combination of the columns of $A$, with the entries of $\mathbf{x}$ as the coefficients.

linear algebra matrix addition.summary: Matrix addition combines two same-size matrices by adding their corresponding entries one by one.

linear algebra lower unitriangular matri.path: linear algebra/linear algebra lower unitriangular matrix.md
linear algebra lower unitriangular matri.summary: A lower unitriangular matrix is a square matrix that has $1$s on the main diagonal, nonzero entries only below the diagonal, and zeros everywhere above.

linear algebra lu decomposition.summary: LU decomposition factors a square matrix into the product of a lower triangular matrix $L$ and an upper triangular matrix $U$, packaging all the steps of Gaussian elimination into two reusable pieces.

linear algebra linear system.summary: A collection of equations where every unknown appears only to the first power and no two unknowns are multiplied together, all of which must be satisfied simultaneously.

linear algebra left and right inverse re.path: linear algebra/linear algebra left and right inverse rectangular matrix.md
linear algebra left and right inverse re.summary: A non-square matrix can have a left inverse or a right inverse, but never both at the same time, which is why only square matrices can have a true two-sided inverse.

linear algebra ldv factorization.summary: The LDV factorization splits a regular matrix into a lower unitriangular factor, a diagonal factor carrying the pivots, and an upper unitriangular factor.

linear algebra ldlt factorization.summary: When a matrix is symmetric and regular, its $LDV$ factorization automatically satisfies $V = L^T$, so you only need to store $L$ and $D$ — the matrix factors into $L D L^T$.
